            # Blueprint-reviewer directive — iter-164 (whole-blueprint audit)

## Focus this iter (do not restrict the audit, but weight these)

The active route is **Route C** (genus-0 base case via Milne §I.3), file
`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`, chapter `AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex`.
iter-163 landed Cor 1.5 (`lem:hom_additivity_over_product`) and Cor 1.2
(`lem:av_regular_map_is_hom`) axiom-clean; those blocks cleared the HARD GATE last iter.

The NEXT prover frontier is the pair:
- `lem:rational_map_to_av_extends` (Milne Thm 3.2 — the codim-1 rational-map-extension
  on a surface; the route's RISKIEST piece; no Lean decl exists yet; flagged in
  `rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap`).
- `lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial` (`morphism_Ga_to_av_const`, Prop 3.9 core; depends on the
  above + the proven Rigidity Lemma; no Lean decl exists yet).

Judge specifically whether THESE TWO blocks are prover-ready:
1. Is the proof of `lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial` detailed enough to formalize? In
   particular the closing "𝔾_a / 𝔾_m incompatibility ⟹ f constant" step (chapter
   lines ~878–885) is currently a one-sentence assertion ("the two relations are
   incompatible ... unless f is constant"). Is that decomposed into a formalizable
   argument, or does it need a blueprint-writer to break it into sub-lemmas (e.g. an
   explicit `Hom(𝔾_a, A) = 0` lemma + how the additive/multiplicative structures
   collide)? Flag the level of infrastructure assumed (a concrete ℙ¹, 𝔾_a/𝔾_m as
   group schemes, the open immersion 𝔾_a ↪ ℙ¹) and whether the chapter says where it
   comes from.
2. Is `lem:rational_map_to_av_extends` stated/proved at a level a prover can act on, or
   is it still an open research question pending the Mathlib-divisor-gap resolution
   (`rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap`)? If the latter, say so plainly — it should NOT be
   marked prover-ready until the pointwise-valuative approach is fleshed out.

## Also check
- The 3 deferred genus-0 scaffold blocks (`prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant`,
  `prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1`, `thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV`) still carry
  proof-block `\leanok` while their Lean bodies are `sorry` — this is a KNOWN infra
  bug in `sync_leanok` (keyword-prefix mismatch; reported separately). Note it if you

  those three as OPEN regardless of the marker.
- `\uses` graph forward-acyclic; any laundering (a `\leanok` proof that secretly
  rests on a `sorry`).

## Output

`## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section if any strategy phase has no coverage.
The planner uses your checklist to decide which chapters get a blueprint-writer this
iter and whether the deep frontier may enter prover objectives.

content: I'll start by reading my full instructions and project context.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown

217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220

222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233

237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.

371	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
372	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
373	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter.
374	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
375	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels.

377	  - **Unstarted-phase proposals** — each proposed chapter lands here as `unstarted-phase proposal: <phase name> — dispatch blueprint-writer for <proposed chapter filename> or record deferral`. These are must-act because an unwritten blueprint is an unparallelisable, unpreviewable phase; writing it early is the cheapest action in the loop.

379	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.

387	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter. Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
9	irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
10	signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.
11	
12	The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
13	assumption. The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally);
14	only `isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. The spine is
15	**pointed vs. unpointed**, not genus-0 vs. positive. `genus C := dim_k H^1(C,O_C)`
16	(arithmetic genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).
17	
18	## Phases & estimations
19	
20	| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
21	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
22	| **Route A — Picard/Quot/Hilbert FGA engine** (positive-genus OBJECT; CRITICAL PATH) | committed, blueprint sketch-level | ~40–70 | ~5100+ · 0/it | Hilbert/Quot repr (Nitsure §4–5); FGA `Pic_{C/k}` repr; `Pic⁰`; Albanese UP | A.2 representability is the riskiest, least-Mathlib piece; project-fatal if it stalls; mandatory regardless (the object is `Pic⁰`) |
23	| **genus-0 rigidity** (object `J=Spec k` trivial) | Rigidity-Lemma chain CLOSED axiom-clean; base-case route DECIDED = Route C (Milne §I.3, char-free end-to-end) | ~15–30 | ~3000–5500 · chain done, base-case 0/it | Cor 1.5 additivity + Cor 1.2 (from proven Rigidity Lemma); rational-map extension via valuative criterion (in Mathlib); `𝔾_a/𝔾_m` + `Hom(𝔾_a,A)=0`; genus-0⟹ℙ¹ RR bridge | surface rational-map-extension gap (Lemma 3.3 codim-1 indeterminacy on `ℙ¹×ℙ¹`, no Mathlib divisors) is on the genus-0 path = riskiest piece; RR bridge has no Mathlib support |
24	| `genusZeroWitness` body + terminal cluster on `Spec k` + **`k̄→k` descent** | gated | 3–5 | 350–850 · gated | terminal cluster on `Spec k`; faithfully-flat descent of a morphism equality along `Spec k̄ → Spec k` | descent direction unconfirmed; skeleton 6/7 fields closed, only the `key` rigidity equation open |
25	| `nonempty_jacobianWitness` genus-stratified body | gated | 1 | <50 · gated | `by_cases h : genus C = 0` | trivial once both arms close |
26	
27	## Routes
28	
29	The positive-genus OBJECT is built by **Route A (Picard scheme via FGA)** —
30	mandatory and essentially unavoidable. The genus-0 arm is a separate, lower-risk
31	rigidity statement (object trivial); its base-case proof route is **Route C (Milne
32	§I.3 rigidity completion)**, chosen because Milne §I.3 + §III.6 derive both the
33	genus-0 base case AND Route A's Albanese UP cube-free, and a Mathlib-support survey
34	found Route C far less blocked than the differential route.
35	
36	### Route A (COMMITTED) — Picard scheme / Albanese via FGA
37	
38	Hilbert/Quot representability + identity-component `Pic⁰` + Albanese universal
39	property. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` § "Route A". Literature in-tree:
40	`references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (§4 existence, §5 `Pic⁰`), `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf`
41	(Quot/Hilbert engine). **Positive-genus arm:** `J := Pic⁰_{C/k}`, a dim-`g`
42	abelian variety; the Albanese UP gives `isAlbaneseFor`. Critical path: the object
43	must be a real dim-`g` abelian variety even when `C(k)=∅`, so the FGA engine is
44	required unconditionally.
45	
46	### Route C (COMMITTED) — genus-0 rigidity completion via Milne §I.3
47	
48	The genus-0 witness OBJECT is the trivial `J = Spec k` (skeleton 6/7 closed); it
49	needs **no** Picard scheme. Remaining content: over `k̄`, every pointed `f : C → A`
50	from a genus-0 curve to an abelian variety is constant. The **Rigidity Lemma**
51	(Mumford Form I = Milne Rigidity Thm 1.1) is PROVEN axiom-clean and is the
52	foundation of this route. Milne §I.3 derives `ℙ¹→A constant` from it with **no
53	theorem of the cube**. Chain:
54	
55	- **Cor 1.5** (additivity): `h : V×W → A`, `h(v₀,w₀)=0 ⟹ h = f∘p + g∘q` uniquely.
56	  Direct from the proven Rigidity Lemma (the difference `h − (f∘p+g∘q)` collapses on
57	  both axes). **No-regret** — Route A's Albanese UP (Milne Prop 6.4) also needs it.
58	- **Cor 1.2** (regular map of AVs sending `0↦0` is a homomorphism). From Cor 1.5.
59	- **Thm 3.2** (rational map from a nonsingular variety to an AV is everywhere
60	  defined). Via the valuative criterion of properness (in Mathlib:
61	  `IsProper.of_valuativeCriterion`, `ValuativeCriterion`) + indeterminacy-locus pure
62	  codim 1 (Milne Lemma 3.3). The surface case (`ℙ¹×ℙ¹`) is the riskiest piece.
63	- **`Hom(𝔾_a,A)=0`** + the `𝔾_a/𝔾_m` incompatibility (Milne Prop 3.9): a homomorphism
64	  from the additive group to an AV is trivial. Char-free.
65	- **genus-0⟹ℙ¹** (RR bridge, Hartshorne IV.1.3.5) — shared, no Mathlib RR.
66	
67	Assembly: Prop 3.9 (`ℙ¹→A const`) collapses the additive-defect map `ψ(x,y)=f(x+y)
68	−f(x)−f(y)` — extended from `𝔾_a×𝔾_a` to the complete surface `ℙ¹×ℙ¹` (Thm 3.2 / Milne
69	Thm 3.4) — by the Rigidity Lemma, then the `𝔾_a/𝔾_m` incompatibility; the headline
70	`rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` = Prop 3.9 + the iso. Route C is uniformly
71	char-free. Survey: `analogies/route-support.md`.
72	
73	### Off-path fallbacks (kept in tree, NOT pursued)
74	
75	- **(c-hybrid) differential + Frobenius**, **(c-cube) theorem of the cube**, **(b)
76	  `dim Pic⁰=0` byproduct of Route A**, **(a) Serre-duality `df=0`** (artifact:
77	  `rigidity_over_kbar` `[CharZero]` in `RigidityKbar.lean`). All rejected/superseded
78	  in favour of Route C — see `iter/iter-163/plan.md` for the comparison.
79	
80	## Open strategic questions
81	
82	- **Base-case route decision — RESOLVED = Route C (Milne §I.3).** Milne §I.3
83	  (Prop 3.9/3.10) and §III.6 (Prop 6.1/6.4, Albanese UP) both derive from the Rigidity
84	  circle (Thm 1.1 + Thm 3.2 + Cor 1.2/1.5) with the theorem of the cube on NEITHER
85	  path. Mathlib-support survey confirmed Route C least blocked.
86	- **Route C's riskiest piece: the rational-map extension surface gap (Milne Lemma 3.3).**
87	  Valuative criterion gives "defined in codim 1"; upgrading to "everywhere defined"
88	  for maps to a group variety needs the pure-codim-1 indeterminacy lemma on the
89	  surface `ℙ¹×ℙ¹`. Mathlib has no Weil-divisor theory — OPEN whether the pointwise
90	  valuative side-step (extend at each codim-1 point) avoids building divisor theory.
91	- **Architecture (settled).** Route-(c) declarations are UPSTREAM of `Jacobian.lean`
92	  (the import cycle `RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian` blocks `genusZeroWitness`
93	  from consuming the keystone). File `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`
94	  (imports `Genus`, imported by `Jacobian`) + its own 1:1 chapter. The old
95	  `rigidity_over_kbar` (in `RigidityKbar.lean`, `[CharZero]`) is the fallback-(a)
96	  artifact, kept in tree.
97	- **Route A representability scheduling.** A.1–A.4 (esp. A.2 Quot/Hilbert repr)
98	  remain the dominant positive-genus cost; flesh to prover-ready blocks (entry:
99	  `RelativeSpec` ~700–1100 LOC) after the genus-0 stack. HARD GATE blocks a prover
100	  until the relevant chapter is complete+correct.
101	- **`k̄→k` descent is route-dependent**, not a fixed cost: a `Pic⁰`-over-`k` argument
102	  may give `Alb(C)=Spec k` directly (no descent); a `C_k̄≅ℙ¹` argument incurs the
103	  faithfully-flat descent of a morphism equality (direction unconfirmed).
104	- Should the chart-algebra envelope + GrpObj cotangent material be split out of the
105	  large `RigidityKbar.tex` into a dedicated off-path chapter (structural job)?
106	
107	## Mathlib gaps & new material
108	
109	**Gaps to fill (CRITICAL PATH — Route A engine, positive-genus object):**
110	
111	- **Hilbert / Quot scheme representability** (Nitsure §4–5) + **FGA `Pic_{C/k}`
112	  representability** — the single highest-cost build, upstream of `Pic⁰`. ~3775 LOC.
113	- **identity-component subgroup scheme** `Pic⁰ ⊆ Pic`; degree map `Pic → ℤ`. ~800.
114	- **Albanese universal property** of `Pic⁰` (`isAlbaneseFor` for `g ≥ 1`): every
115	  `f : C → A` killing `P` factors uniquely through `Pic⁰`. Goal-required; uses the
116	  Rigidity Lemma + Cor 1.2/1.5 + Thm 3.2 (Milne Prop 6.1/6.4) — **NOT the theorem of
117	  the cube** (Milne §III.6). Shares Route C's Cor 1.5/Cor 1.2/Thm 3.2
118	  infrastructure. NOT yet itemised in ~5100 — true budget higher.
119	
120	**Gaps to fill (genus-0 rigidity — Route C, Milne §I.3):**
121	
122	- **Rigidity Lemma** (Mumford Form I §4 = Milne Thm 1.1; cube-free) — **DONE, axiom-clean**. Foundation of Route C AND Route A's Albanese UP.
123	- **Cor 1.5 (additivity) + Cor 1.2 (AV maps are homs)** — direct corollaries of the
124	  proven Rigidity Lemma; the next concrete targets. No-regret (Albanese UP needs them).
125	- **Rational-map extension to an AV** (Milne Thm 3.2 / Lemma 3.3) — valuative
126	  criterion present in Mathlib (`IsProper.of_valuativeCriterion`); the codim-1
127	  indeterminacy upgrade is the contained gap (no Mathlib Weil divisors).
128	- **`𝔾_a`/`𝔾_m` group structures + `Hom(𝔾_a,A)=0`** — `AffineSpace 𝔸(n;S)` + `GrpObj`
129	  present in Mathlib; the additive-group structure on `𝔸¹` and the triviality lemma
130	  are to build (one lemma over existing API).
131	- **genus-0 + k̄-point ⟹ `≅ ℙ¹`** (Hartshorne IV.1.3.5) — Riemann–Roch-flavoured
132	  sub-build (no Mathlib RR). Shared / needed by Route C.
133	- No `AbelianVariety` theory and no general Riemann–Roch in Mathlib (both verified
134	  absent).
135	
136	**Gaps to fill (FALLBACK route (a) only — off critical path):**
137	
138	- `df = 0` for general genus-0: `Ω_A` triviality + **Serre duality** `H^0(C,Ω_C)=0`
139	  (~3000–8000; no Mathlib dualizing sheaf / RR / genus↔Ω bridge). The chart
140	  `Ω_C(V)` is free rank-1, so the KDM stack cannot detect `df=0`. (c-hybrid)
141	  supersedes this by routing through the concrete `ℙ¹`.
142	
143	**New project material introduced (in tree):**
144	
145	- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean` — the route-(c) AV-rigidity stack
146	  (Rigidity Lemma → base case → genus-0-curve-to-AV), UPSTREAM of `Jacobian.lean`;
147	  own chapter. Consumed by `genusZeroWitness.key`.
148	- `Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` — chart-algebra envelope (KDM, constants,
149	  `df_zero_factors`, thin `ext_of_diff_zero`). CLOSED, axiom-clean. Reused by
150	  (c-hybrid) / fallback (a).
151	- `Cotangent/GrpObj.lean` — `cotangentSpaceAtIdentity` trio; `shearMulRight`.
152	  Design template for the `Ω_A` globalization in (c-hybrid).
153	- `Rigidity.lean` `ext_of_eqOnOpen` — dominant-source / separated-target rigidity
154	  packaging; consumed by the scheme-lift + Albanese uniqueness.
155	
156	**Soundness rules (operational):** No new axioms (every closed decl `lean_verify`s
157	to `propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`). A `sorry` must be the body of a
158	top-level named declaration — never buried in a `letI`/`have`/anonymous-`fun`

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
32	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
33	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
34	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
35	derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube, through a short
36	additive-structure chain: additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
37	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5); the fact that a pointed regular
38	map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne
39	Corollary~1.2); the everywhere-definedness of a rational map from a nonsingular variety into
40	an abelian variety (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}, Milne Theorem~3.2); and the
41	$\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility that forces a pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ to be
42	constant (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, the core of Milne Proposition~3.9). Combining these
43	gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
44	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
45	curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
46	headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
47	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
48	neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
49	fully decoupled from Route~A.
50	
51	\medskip
52	
53	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
54	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
55	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
56	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
57	
58	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
59	
60	\begin{lemma}
61	
62	\leanok
63	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
64	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
66	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
68	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
69	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
70	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
71	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
72	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
73	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
74	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
75	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
76	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
77	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
78	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
82	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
83	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
84	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
85	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
86	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
87	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
88	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
89	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
90	% assertion."

107	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:

109	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
110	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
111	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
112	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual

114	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
115	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
116	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
117	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
118	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
119	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
120	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.

122	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
123	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
124	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
125	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
126	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
127	
128	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
129	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
130	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
131	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
132	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
133	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
134	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
135	
136	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
137	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
138	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
139	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
140	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
141	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
142	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
143	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
144	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
145	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
146	\end{proof}

148	\begin{remark}
149	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
150	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
151	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
152	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
153	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
154	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
155	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
156	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
157	  irreducible.)
158	\end{remark}

207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
211	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
213	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
214	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
215	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
216	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
217	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
218	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
219	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
220	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
221	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
222	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
223	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
224	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
225	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
226	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
227	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
228	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
229	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
230	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
231	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
232	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
233	
234	  \medskip
235	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
236	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
237	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
238	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
239	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
240	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
241	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
242	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
243	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
244	
245	  \medskip
246	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
247	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
248	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
249	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
250	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of
251	  the affine $U_0$; this requires $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. Second, the globalisation of the
252	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
253	  closed points of the saturated open $U$ to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ $U$ to be a Jacobson space.
254	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of
255	  finite type} over $\bar k$ (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
256	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
257	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$. Accordingly the formalization carries
258	  \emph{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
259	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to
260	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
261	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
262	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
263	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
264	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
265	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
266	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$ ---
267	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over $\bar k$, so the
268	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
269	\end{lemma}

281	\begin{proof}
282	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
283	  \leanok
284	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
285	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
286	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
287	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
288	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
289	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
290	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
291	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
292	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
293	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
294	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
295	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
296	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
297	
298	  \medskip
299	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
300	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
301	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
302	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
303	
304	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``$X$ complete $\Rightarrow p_2$ closed'' is
305	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
306	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
307	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
308	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
309	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
310	  proven.
311	
312	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of $V$) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
313	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
314	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
315	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
316	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
317	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
318	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and
319	  $s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0$ come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
320	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
321	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
322	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
323	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.
324	
325	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
326	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
327	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
328	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
329	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
330	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
331	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
332	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
333	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
334	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
335	  extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring
336	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so $f$ and
337	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
338	  locally-of-finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
339	  locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
340	  hypothesis $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$), so the per-slice
341	  agreement globalises to all of $U$ via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
342	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
343	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
344	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
345	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
346	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
347	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
348	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
349	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
350	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
351	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
352	\end{proof}

355	
356	\leanok
357	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
358	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}

378	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
379	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible, reduced,
380	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over $\bar k$ (the formalization carries
381	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$, which makes the saturated open $U$
382	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
383	  consumes), and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
384	  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
385	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)$ for the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
386	  endomorphism. Suppose:
387	  \begin{itemize}

389	      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
390	      its points);
391	    \item $U_0 \subseteq Z$ is an \emph{affine} open;
392	    \item $f(U) \subseteq U_0$, i.e.\ $f$ maps $U$ into the affine $U_0$.
393	  \end{itemize}
394	  Then $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on $U$ as scheme morphisms:
395	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$.

398	\begin{proof}
399	
400	  \uses{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
401	  \leanok
402	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
403	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
404	  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
405	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
406	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
407	  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
408	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.

410	  \medskip
411	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
412	  Fix a closed point $x$ of $U$, lying over a closed point $y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
413	  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
414	  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
415	  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
416	  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
417	  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
418	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
419	  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
420	  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
421	  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
422	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of $U$. This per-slice statement is the
423	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
424	
425	  \medskip
426	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
427	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$
428	  ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
429	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$). To turn ``$f$ and
430	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on $U$,
431	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
432	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
433	  $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense --- and feeds
434	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (the same
435	  family $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses). This yields the morphism equality
436	  $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$ on all of $U$. The
437	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
438	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
439	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); that bespoke build is now done in
440	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
441	\end{proof}
